title: std::thread — create threads
instruction:
`#include <thread>` to use `std::thread`.

- `thread t(function, args...)` — starts a thread
- `.join()` — wait for thread to finish
- `.detach()` — let it run independently

Always join or detach before the thread object is destroyed.
successMessage: Two threads running concurrently — output order varies. join() blocks main until the thread finishes. Never let a thread object be destroyed without join() or detach() — that calls std::terminate().

title: mutex — prevent race conditions
instruction:
Multiple threads accessing shared data simultaneously causes race conditions.

`mutex::lock()`/`unlock()` — manual (error-prone).
`lock_guard<mutex>` — RAII: locks on construction, unlocks on destruction.
successMessage: lock_guard acquires the mutex in the constructor, releases it in the destructor — RAII lock management. Without it, sharedCounter would almost never be 10000. lock_guard is safe even if an exception is thrown.

title: atomic — lock-free operations
instruction:
`atomic<T>` provides thread-safe operations without mutex overhead for simple types:

```cpp
atomic<int> counter{0};
counter++;              // thread-safe increment
counter.fetch_add(5);   // thread-safe add
```

Faster than mutex for single-variable access.
successMessage: atomic<int> is always correct — hardware-level atomic operations. unsafeCounter is almost never 10000. Use atomic for simple counters, flags, and pointers. Use mutex for more complex shared state.

title: async and future — task parallelism
instruction:
`std::async` runs a function asynchronously and returns a `future<T>`.
`future::get()` blocks until the result is ready:

```cpp
auto f = async(launch::async, []{ return expensiveCalc(); });
int result = f.get();  // waits if not done
```
successMessage: Both Fibonacci calculations ran concurrently — f1.get() blocked until 38 was done. On a multi-core CPU, this is roughly twice as fast as sequential. async is the high-level tool for easy parallelism.

title: condition_variable — coordinate threads
instruction:
`condition_variable` lets threads wait for a condition and notify others:

```cpp
condition_variable cv;
cv.wait(lock, []{ return dataReady; });  // wait until dataReady
cv.notify_one();  // wake one waiter
cv.notify_all();  // wake all waiters
```

This is how producer-consumer patterns work.
successMessage: Producer-consumer pattern with condition_variable. cv.wait() releases the lock while waiting, reacquires it when woken. This is the foundation of thread pools, message queues, and event systems.

title: Build a parallel sum
instruction:
No starter code.

Write a parallel sum function:
1. Split a large vector into N chunks (N = thread count)
2. Each thread sums its chunk and stores to a `vector<long long> partialSums`
3. After all threads join, sum the partial sums
4. Verify it equals the sequential sum

Test with a vector of 1,000,000 integers (1 to 1,000,000).
hint: Create threads[i] = thread([&, i]{ partialSums[i] = accumulate(start, end, 0LL); }); where start/end are iterators to the chunk.

codeChecks.message: Use std::thread or async for parallel execution.
Use a vector of 1 million elements.

successMessage: Parallel summation — N threads work simultaneously, then results are combined. On a quad-core CPU this is ~4x faster than sequential. Parallel algorithms like this are in <execution> in C++17. Chapter 22 done — C++ complete!
